title: SDK: add batchingTier option (fast/standard/cost_efficient) + generated types
preview:
## Summary
Adds an explicit `batchingTier` option to the TS SDK so customers can opt into the new workload-aware batching SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost_efficient) on a per-request basis. This matches the python SDK addition and the server-side rollout plan (initially opt-in via header).

## API surface
- New optional request option: `batchingTier?: BatchingTier`
- `BatchingTier` is a string union: `"fast" | "standard" | "cost_efficient"`
- When set, the SDK sends `x-redwood-batching-tier: <tier>`.
- If omitted, the SDK does not send the header and server-side defaults apply.

Example:
```ts
import { Redwood } from "@redwood-inference/sdk";

const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY! });

const res = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Summarize this…" }],
  batchingTier: "standard",
});
```

## Notes / behavior
- This is intentionally request-scoped only (no global default yet) to avoid surprising customers during rollout.
- Header name aligns with control-plane policy precedence work (customer override > route policy > tier default).
- Validation is performed client-side (compile-time via union type + runtime guard for unknown strings when using JS).

## Implementation details
- Plumbed the option through `chat.completions.create` and `generate.create` request paths.
- Added a small serializer helper so we do not emit the header when undefined.
- Regenerated `src/types/generated.ts` from the OpenAPI snapshot.

## Tests
- Added request serialization test to assert header presence/absence and exact spelling.

## Rollout
- Safe to ship independent of server runtime; server will ignore the header until feature flag is enabled.

---
### Review conversation (highlights)
**Henry Cho:** Can we avoid introducing a breaking rename (e.g., `cost-efficient` vs `cost_efficient`) and keep it consistent with backend enum? Also please add a doc snippet.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Backend enum uses `cost_efficient` (snake) for config compatibility; updated README and examples to use the same. Added a short section explaining values.

**Ethan Park:** Please confirm the header casing/format matches gateway normalization. We’ve had issues with mixed casing.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Confirmed with gateway: we normalize to lowercase on the wire; using `x-redwood-batching-tier` consistently. Added a unit test to lock it.

**Benji Okafor:** Can we ensure we don’t accidentally send an empty header when users pass `""`?

**Jordan Lee (author):** Added runtime guard: empty string is rejected with a clear error message. JS users will see `Invalid batchingTier`.

**Nikhil Sharma:** This is good. Please link to the public docs PR once it lands.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Linked in README note as TODO; will update in follow-up after docs merge.

Approvals: Henry Cho ✅, Ethan Park ✅, Benji Okafor ✅, Nikhil Sharma ✅

## Checklist
- [x] Types generated and checked in
- [x] README + example updated
- [x] Unit test added
- [x] Changeset added

Related:
- Python SDK: redwood-sdk-python#1190
- Runtime tiered defaults rollout: ENG-18422 / PM-612
text:
## Summary
Adds an explicit `batchingTier` option to the TS SDK so customers can opt into the new workload-aware batching SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost_efficient) on a per-request basis. This matches the python SDK addition and the server-side rollout plan (initially opt-in via header).

## API surface
- New optional request option: `batchingTier?: BatchingTier`
- `BatchingTier` is a string union: `"fast" | "standard" | "cost_efficient"`
- When set, the SDK sends `x-redwood-batching-tier: <tier>`.
- If omitted, the SDK does not send the header and server-side defaults apply.

Example:
```ts
import { Redwood } from "@redwood-inference/sdk";

const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY! });

const res = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Summarize this…" }],
  batchingTier: "standard",
});
```

## Notes / behavior
- This is intentionally request-scoped only (no global default yet) to avoid surprising customers during rollout.
- Header name aligns with control-plane policy precedence work (customer override > route policy > tier default).
- Validation is performed client-side (compile-time via union type + runtime guard for unknown strings when using JS).

## Implementation details
- Plumbed the option through `chat.completions.create` and `generate.create` request paths.
- Added a small serializer helper so we do not emit the header when undefined.
- Regenerated `src/types/generated.ts` from the OpenAPI snapshot.

## Tests
- Added request serialization test to assert header presence/absence and exact spelling.

## Rollout
- Safe to ship independent of server runtime; server will ignore the header until feature flag is enabled.

---
### Review conversation (highlights)
**Henry Cho:** Can we avoid introducing a breaking rename (e.g., `cost-efficient` vs `cost_efficient`) and keep it consistent with backend enum? Also please add a doc snippet.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Backend enum uses `cost_efficient` (snake) for config compatibility; updated README and examples to use the same. Added a short section explaining values.

**Ethan Park:** Please confirm the header casing/format matches gateway normalization. We’ve had issues with mixed casing.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Confirmed with gateway: we normalize to lowercase on the wire; using `x-redwood-batching-tier` consistently. Added a unit test to lock it.

**Benji Okafor:** Can we ensure we don’t accidentally send an empty header when users pass `""`?

**Jordan Lee (author):** Added runtime guard: empty string is rejected with a clear error message. JS users will see `Invalid batchingTier`.

**Nikhil Sharma:** This is good. Please link to the public docs PR once it lands.

**Jordan Lee (author):** Linked in README note as TODO; will update in follow-up after docs merge.

Approvals: Henry Cho ✅, Ethan Park ✅, Benji Okafor ✅, Nikhil Sharma ✅

## Checklist
- [x] Types generated and checked in
- [x] README + example updated
- [x] Unit test added
- [x] Changeset added

Related:
- Python SDK: redwood-sdk-python#1190
- Runtime tiered defaults rollout: ENG-18422 / PM-612

title: Introduce delegated token channel and unified refresh for pull/push streams (ergonomic parity)
preview: Motivation: There has been persistent cross-SDK divergence in how streaming consumers handle credential refresh and retries, resulting in token rotation races, duplicated refresh attempts, and awkward ergonomics when migrating examples between TypeScript, Go, and Python. This PR introduces a delegated token channel abstraction in the TypeScript SDK that matches the semantics we iterated on in Go/Python: single-flight refresh, lease-window proactive refresh, and explicit channel handoff for pull and push style streams. Summary of changes: 1) src/auth/delegated_channel.ts: new DelegatedTokenChannel class that accepts a user-provided async refresh handler and exposes a lightweight getToken()/invalidate() API optimized for streaming hot-paths. 2) src/stream/pullstream_adapter.ts and src/stream/pushstream_adapter.ts: adapters updated to consume DelegatedTokenChannel; removed inline refresh logic and consolidated retry logic into a shared stream/retry shim. 3) src/client/stream_client.ts: ergonomic convenience helpers StreamClient.openPullStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() and openPushStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() to reduce boilerplate for common patterns. 4) docs/auth/stream-credential-flow.md: documented the new pattern and migration notes. 5) tests/integration/stream_token_rotation.test.ts: deterministic test harness that simulates short-lived tokens and asserts single-flight refresh under concurrent consumers. 6) package.json bumped to 0.8.0 to reflect API additions. Compatibility and migration: The DelegatedTokenChannel is fully backward compatible with existing tokenProvider callback patterns but is recommended for streaming workloads. We maintained the legacy token callback wrapper (deprecated) and added deprecation warnings for one release cycle. Performance: stream hot-paths were benchmarked; added microbench in perf/bench_stream_token_hotpath.ts showing ~8% lower allocs and ~6% lower p95 latency when using delegated channel due to removed promise duplication. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed with SDK owners (ENG-9345) - [x] Type-safe APIs with TS declarations and JSDoc - [x] Integration tests covering race conditions - [x] Docs and quickstart updated - [x] Version bump and changelog entry - [x] CI green after two reruns Test notes: initial CI run showed a flaky integration test that reproduced locally when environment timeouts were aggressive; adjusted harness timing and re-ran full suite. Observability: added telemetry events auth.refresh.requested/auth.refresh.completed for monitoring refresh count per-client to make regressions visible in Console. Rollout plan: merged to main behind feature-flag default-on for SDK 0.8.x; recommend library consumers to adopt DelegatedTokenChannel for streaming jobs and migrate examples. Related: aligns TypeScript behavior with redwood-sdk-go@v1.5.3 and redwood-sdk-python@0.12.4 token lease semantics.
text: Motivation: There has been persistent cross-SDK divergence in how streaming consumers handle credential refresh and retries, resulting in token rotation races, duplicated refresh attempts, and awkward ergonomics when migrating examples between TypeScript, Go, and Python. This PR introduces a delegated token channel abstraction in the TypeScript SDK that matches the semantics we iterated on in Go/Python: single-flight refresh, lease-window proactive refresh, and explicit channel handoff for pull and push style streams. Summary of changes: 1) src/auth/delegated_channel.ts: new DelegatedTokenChannel class that accepts a user-provided async refresh handler and exposes a lightweight getToken()/invalidate() API optimized for streaming hot-paths. 2) src/stream/pullstream_adapter.ts and src/stream/pushstream_adapter.ts: adapters updated to consume DelegatedTokenChannel; removed inline refresh logic and consolidated retry logic into a shared stream/retry shim. 3) src/client/stream_client.ts: ergonomic convenience helpers StreamClient.openPullStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() and openPushStreamWithDelegatedRefresh() to reduce boilerplate for common patterns. 4) docs/auth/stream-credential-flow.md: documented the new pattern and migration notes. 5) tests/integration/stream_token_rotation.test.ts: deterministic test harness that simulates short-lived tokens and asserts single-flight refresh under concurrent consumers. 6) package.json bumped to 0.8.0 to reflect API additions. Compatibility and migration: The DelegatedTokenChannel is fully backward compatible with existing tokenProvider callback patterns but is recommended for streaming workloads. We maintained the legacy token callback wrapper (deprecated) and added deprecation warnings for one release cycle. Performance: stream hot-paths were benchmarked; added microbench in perf/bench_stream_token_hotpath.ts showing ~8% lower allocs and ~6% lower p95 latency when using delegated channel due to removed promise duplication. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed with SDK owners (ENG-9345) - [x] Type-safe APIs with TS declarations and JSDoc - [x] Integration tests covering race conditions - [x] Docs and quickstart updated - [x] Version bump and changelog entry - [x] CI green after two reruns Test notes: initial CI run showed a flaky integration test that reproduced locally when environment timeouts were aggressive; adjusted harness timing and re-ran full suite. Observability: added telemetry events auth.refresh.requested/auth.refresh.completed for monitoring refresh count per-client to make regressions visible in Console. Rollout plan: merged to main behind feature-flag default-on for SDK 0.8.x; recommend library consumers to adopt DelegatedTokenChannel for streaming jobs and migrate examples. Related: aligns TypeScript behavior with redwood-sdk-go@v1.5.3 and redwood-sdk-python@0.12.4 token lease semantics.

title: feat(usage): add billed token + experiment cohort fields to Usage API client
preview: Context: Finance/RevOps + Product are analyzing Q1 pricing/packaging experiments and hardening metering. The backend Usage API now returns additional dimensions needed for (a) fair customer explanations (billed vs raw tokens, cache attribution) and (b) experiment analysis (cohort/variant attribution). This PR updates the TypeScript SDK to expose those fields in a typed, backwards-compatible way. Changes: (1) Update Usage API response types to include raw token fields and billed token fields (rawPromptTokens/rawCompletionTokens/rawTokensTotal + billedPromptTokens/billedCompletionTokens/billedTokensTotal) and cache attribution (cacheHitTokens when returned by API). (2) Add experiment attribution fields to usage line items: experimentKey, experimentVariant, experimentCohort. These map 1:1 to the API payload when present; when the account/day wasn’t part of an experiment or the server doesn’t emit these dimensions, they remain undefined. (3) Update the usage client deserialization so snake_case API fields map to camelCase SDK fields (e.g., billed_prompt_tokens -> billedPromptTokens). (4) Update docs/snippets in the README for usage exports to call out billed vs raw tokens and the experiment fields as optional dimensions. Example usage: const rows = await client.usage.list({ start: '2026-02-01', end: '2026-02-02', groupBy: ['day','model','region'] }); for (const r of rows.data) { console.log(r.billedTokensTotal, r.rawTokensTotal, r.experimentVariant); } Backwards compatibility: additive only; existing consumers compiling against older fields continue to work. The runtime behavior is unchanged; this is purely surfacing additional fields returned by the API. Testing: added unit tests around usage response parsing (covers missing fields, partial presence, and numeric coercion), and ran the examples against staging with a known experiment account. Notes for rollout: publish as a minor version bump after the server-side Usage API field rollout is at 100% (server is already gated). Links: aligns with the ongoing metering hardening + experiment analysis work (PM-1848) and addresses customer confusion seen in SUP-28451 (invoice vs usage export mismatch) by making billed token fields first-class in the SDK.
text: Context: Finance/RevOps + Product are analyzing Q1 pricing/packaging experiments and hardening metering. The backend Usage API now returns additional dimensions needed for (a) fair customer explanations (billed vs raw tokens, cache attribution) and (b) experiment analysis (cohort/variant attribution). This PR updates the TypeScript SDK to expose those fields in a typed, backwards-compatible way. Changes: (1) Update Usage API response types to include raw token fields and billed token fields (rawPromptTokens/rawCompletionTokens/rawTokensTotal + billedPromptTokens/billedCompletionTokens/billedTokensTotal) and cache attribution (cacheHitTokens when returned by API). (2) Add experiment attribution fields to usage line items: experimentKey, experimentVariant, experimentCohort. These map 1:1 to the API payload when present; when the account/day wasn’t part of an experiment or the server doesn’t emit these dimensions, they remain undefined. (3) Update the usage client deserialization so snake_case API fields map to camelCase SDK fields (e.g., billed_prompt_tokens -> billedPromptTokens). (4) Update docs/snippets in the README for usage exports to call out billed vs raw tokens and the experiment fields as optional dimensions. Example usage: const rows = await client.usage.list({ start: '2026-02-01', end: '2026-02-02', groupBy: ['day','model','region'] }); for (const r of rows.data) { console.log(r.billedTokensTotal, r.rawTokensTotal, r.experimentVariant); } Backwards compatibility: additive only; existing consumers compiling against older fields continue to work. The runtime behavior is unchanged; this is purely surfacing additional fields returned by the API. Testing: added unit tests around usage response parsing (covers missing fields, partial presence, and numeric coercion), and ran the examples against staging with a known experiment account. Notes for rollout: publish as a minor version bump after the server-side Usage API field rollout is at 100% (server is already gated). Links: aligns with the ongoing metering hardening + experiment analysis work (PM-1848) and addresses customer confusion seen in SUP-28451 (invoice vs usage export mismatch) by making billed token fields first-class in the SDK.

title: Add request ID capture + logging helper for faster Support escalations
preview:
## Summary
Support escalations frequently stall because customers don’t include request IDs, or because the SDK drops the `x-request-id` header on error paths. This PR adds a small, opt-in helper that (a) captures Redwood request IDs from responses and error responses, (b) exposes them on thrown SDK errors, and (c) provides a safe logging hook so customers can copy/paste request IDs into Support tickets.

This is intentionally not enabled by default to avoid surprising logging behavior in customer apps.

## Motivation
- Support intake + escalation playbook now standardizes on collecting request IDs early (see linked SUP tickets).
- Streaming disconnects/timeouts are hard to reproduce without request IDs.
- Customers often ask “where do I find the request ID?” and we want a canonical answer in the TS SDK.

## What changed
- Added `requestId` extraction utilities (from headers) and an opt-in hook `onRequestId`.
- Updated SDK error shaping so thrown errors preserve a `requestId` field when present.
- Updated docs and examples to show how to log/capture request IDs without logging full payloads.

### Public API
- `getRequestIdFromHeaders(headers: Headers | Record<string, string | string[] | undefined>): string | undefined`
- `createRequestIdLogger({ onRequestId })` -> returns a small helper to attach to SDK request pipeline

No behavior changes unless consumers opt in by passing `onRequestId`.

## Implementation notes
- We normalize header lookups for Node (undici / fetch) and browser environments.
- We parse request ID from:
  - `x-request-id` (canonical)
  - `x-redwood-request-id` (legacy alias; kept for compatibility)
- Errors thrown by the SDK now include:
  - `requestId?: string`
  - `status?: number`
  - `code?: string`

## Example usage
```ts
import { Redwood, createRequestIdLogger } from "@redwood-inference/sdk";

const { onResponse } = createRequestIdLogger({
  onRequestId: (requestId) => {
    // Safe to log: request ID only (no prompts / payloads)
    console.info("Redwood requestId=", requestId);
  },
});

const client = new Redwood({
  apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY!,
  hooks: {
    onResponse,
  },
});

try {
  const res = await client.chat.completions.create({
    model: "redwood/verified-llama",
    messages: [{ role: "user", content: "hello" }],
    stream: false,
  });
} catch (err: any) {
  console.log("Support requestId:", err.requestId);
  throw err;
}
```

## Files changed (high level)
- `src/support/requestId.ts` (new): header normalization + extraction
- `src/support/requestIdLogger.ts` (new): opt-in hook wrapper
- `src/core/errors.ts`: preserve `requestId` on SDK error type
- `src/core/http.ts`: call request-id capture on both success + error responses
- `README.md`: troubleshooting section update
- `examples/node/request-id-logging.ts` (new)
- `test/requestId.test.ts` (new)

### Illustrative snippet
```ts
export function getRequestIdFromHeaders(headers: Headers | Record<string, unknown>): string | undefined {
  const h = normalizeHeaders(headers);
  return (h["x-request-id"] as string | undefined) ?? (h["x-redwood-request-id"] as string | undefined);
}
```

## Validation
- Added unit tests covering:
  - browser `Headers`
  - Node/undici response headers normalization
  - error response paths (4xx/5xx)
  - legacy header alias
- Manual: ran `examples/node/request-id-logging.ts` and confirmed request IDs print for both successful and forced-401 scenarios.

## Rollout / customer guidance
- Support can reference this for customers who ask how to find request IDs.
- Follow-up: we should cross-link this from the streaming troubleshooting doc update (ENG-4827) once merged.

## CI
- ✅ lint
- ✅ typecheck
- ✅ unit tests
- ✅ build

## Review conversation highlights
- Owen Phillips: requested we keep logging opt-in and avoid default console logging. Updated to require explicit `onRequestId` callback and clarified in README.
- Priya Shah: asked that requestId be present on thrown errors to support copy/paste into SUP tickets. Added `requestId?: string` to the public error surface and ensured it’s set on non-2xx responses.
- Ethan Park: flagged header normalization differences between environments. Added normalization helper + tests for both `Headers` and plain objects.

## Links
- Support tickets: SUP-1842, SUP-1854
- Workstreams: PM-914, ENG-4827

Merged via squash.

This PR adds a small, opt-in helper that (a) captures Redwood request IDs from responses and error responses, (b) exposes them on thrown SDK errors, and (c) provides a safe logging hook so customers can copy/paste request IDs into Support tickets.

## Example usage

title: Retry-After parsing (delta-seconds + HTTP-date) + jittered exponential backoff
preview: Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative (retry/timeout standard), TS currently only parses Retry-After when it is a delta-seconds value. The gateway and some upstreams also emit Retry-After as an HTTP-date (e.g. "Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT"), which we should respect to avoid retrying too aggressively during throttling. In addition, our retry middleware used a deterministic exponential backoff sequence, which can create synchronized retries (thundering herd) across many clients. This PR adds: (1) Retry-After parsing for both delta-seconds and HTTP-date formats, (2) jittered exponential backoff consistent with the parity standard, and (3) tests/fixtures to lock behavior. Behavior details: Retry-After parsing: If header is delta-seconds => wait = clamp(deltaSeconds*1000, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If header is HTTP-date => wait = clamp(dateMs - nowMs, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If parsing fails or results in NaN/negative => treat as absent and fall back to exponential backoff. We only honor Retry-After on status codes where we retry (429, 500/502/503/504), with primary focus on 429. Backoff + jitter: Introduce a jittered exponential backoff helper (baseDelayMs=250, multiplier=2, maxDelayMs=10_000) using full jitter: delay = randomInt(0..cap) where cap = min(maxDelayMs, baseDelayMs * 2^(attempt-1)). This matches the parity guidance and reduces retry synchronization under load. Backwards compatibility: No API surface changes; retry defaults remain the same maxAttempts. Only changes are (a) respecting additional valid Retry-After values and (b) adding jitter to the sleep, which may slightly change exact retry timing but improves reliability at scale. Testing: Adds unit tests for Retry-After parsing (seconds, HTTP-date, invalid, negative/0, far-future clamp) and for backoff jitter bounds. Also adds an integration-style test against the retry middleware with a simulated 429 + Retry-After HTTP-date to validate we wait at least the computed duration (with deterministic clock + stubbed RNG). Notes: This does not change streaming retry behavior (still conservative) and does not add any telemetry fields; those are tracked separately under ENG-5129.
text: Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative (retry/timeout standard), TS currently only parses Retry-After when it is a delta-seconds value. The gateway and some upstreams also emit Retry-After as an HTTP-date (e.g. "Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT"), which we should respect to avoid retrying too aggressively during throttling. In addition, our retry middleware used a deterministic exponential backoff sequence, which can create synchronized retries (thundering herd) across many clients. This PR adds: (1) Retry-After parsing for both delta-seconds and HTTP-date formats, (2) jittered exponential backoff consistent with the parity standard, and (3) tests/fixtures to lock behavior. Behavior details: Retry-After parsing: If header is delta-seconds => wait = clamp(deltaSeconds*1000, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If header is HTTP-date => wait = clamp(dateMs - nowMs, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If parsing fails or results in NaN/negative => treat as absent and fall back to exponential backoff. We only honor Retry-After on status codes where we retry (429, 500/502/503/504), with primary focus on 429. Backoff + jitter: Introduce a jittered exponential backoff helper (baseDelayMs=250, multiplier=2, maxDelayMs=10_000) using full jitter: delay = randomInt(0..cap) where cap = min(maxDelayMs, baseDelayMs * 2^(attempt-1)). This matches the parity guidance and reduces retry synchronization under load. Backwards compatibility: No API surface changes; retry defaults remain the same maxAttempts. Only changes are (a) respecting additional valid Retry-After values and (b) adding jitter to the sleep, which may slightly change exact retry timing but improves reliability at scale. Testing: Adds unit tests for Retry-After parsing (seconds, HTTP-date, invalid, negative/0, far-future clamp) and for backoff jitter bounds. Also adds an integration-style test against the retry middleware with a simulated 429 + Retry-After HTTP-date to validate we wait at least the computed duration (with deterministic clock + stubbed RNG). Notes: This does not change streaming retry behavior (still conservative) and does not add any telemetry fields; those are tracked separately under ENG-5129.

title: Unify retry middleware and error shapes (request_id + retryable) with SDK standard
preview:
Context
We’re in the middle of the SDK parity initiative and TS currently differs from Python/Go in two customer-visible ways:
1) Retry classification: TS retry middleware has been overly permissive (observed retries on some terminal 4xx), which contributed to SUP-29438 (duplicate billing concern).
2) Error surfaces: request_id is inconsistently propagated (INT-1182), and error types differ between streaming/non-streaming paths.

This PR refactors the retry middleware and error construction so TS matches the standard taxonomy and aligns retryable status codes. Retry-After parsing/backoff tuning will be handled in follow-up PRs (see PR-1307).

What changed
- Introduced a single error normalization layer used by both streaming + non-streaming requests:
  - All thrown errors extend RedwoodError and include:
    - requestId (from x-redwood-request-id/x-request-id header, with body fallback)
    - statusCode (when available)
    - code (gateway error code when present)
    - retryable (computed)
- Refactored retry middleware to depend on the normalized error shape instead of ad-hoc checks.
- Updated retryable classification to match the SDK retry/timeout standard:
  - Retryable: 408, 409, 429, 500–599 (excluding 501 by default; see note below)
  - Non-retryable: other 4xx (400/401/403/404/etc)
  - Network/transport errors are retryable (DNS, connection reset, TLS handshake, fetch abort not initiated by user)
- Request ID propagation:
  - Successful responses: the Response wrapper exposes requestId.
  - Error responses: requestId is extracted from headers first, then JSON body fields (request_id/requestId) if present.
- Observability hooks:
  - Retry loop now increments a local attempt counter and attaches it to the error as metadata when we eventually give up.
  - Ensures we do NOT include the Authorization header or API key in any error message (Security review guidance).

Notes / decisions
- 501 handling: The standard doc calls out 5xx generally as retryable; SRE feedback was to treat 501 as non-retryable by default (usually indicates unsupported feature/method rather than transient). This PR implements that as a conservative default; we can revisit once gateway error codes are fully consistent.
- Abort semantics: When the caller’s AbortSignal triggers, we now classify it as non-retryable and preserve the original AbortError name/message.

Testing
- Added unit tests for shouldRetry(): verifies status code matrix and network error classification.
- Added tests ensuring request_id is extracted from:
  - response headers (preferred)
  - JSON error body (fallback)
- Updated existing integration tests to assert error.retryable and error.requestId are present.

Rollout
- This should be non-breaking for most customers: existing code catching Error will continue to work.
- Some customers may have relied on the previous (incorrect) retry-on-400 behavior; this change is intentional and documented in migration notes (tracked in ENG-5128).

Follow-ups
- Retry-After parsing + backoff jitter alignment (PR-1307)
- Timeout defaults + AbortController plumbing (PR-1302)
- Conformance suite fixtures (ENG-5127)

Screenshots/log samples
- Example error (sanitized):
  name=RedwoodAPIError statusCode=429 retryable=true requestId=req_01HR9Q6K9YQG3H2R9M4J7V0Z1A code=RATE_LIMITED
text:
Context
We’re in the middle of the SDK parity initiative and TS currently differs from Python/Go in two customer-visible ways:
1) Retry classification: TS retry middleware has been overly permissive (observed retries on some terminal 4xx), which contributed to SUP-29438 (duplicate billing concern).
2) Error surfaces: request_id is inconsistently propagated (INT-1182), and error types differ between streaming/non-streaming paths.

This PR refactors the retry middleware and error construction so TS matches the standard taxonomy and aligns retryable status codes. Retry-After parsing/backoff tuning will be handled in follow-up PRs (see PR-1307).

What changed
- Introduced a single error normalization layer used by both streaming + non-streaming requests:
  - All thrown errors extend RedwoodError and include:
    - requestId (from x-redwood-request-id/x-request-id header, with body fallback)
    - statusCode (when available)
    - code (gateway error code when present)
    - retryable (computed)
- Refactored retry middleware to depend on the normalized error shape instead of ad-hoc checks.
- Updated retryable classification to match the SDK retry/timeout standard:
  - Retryable: 408, 409, 429, 500–599 (excluding 501 by default; see note below)
  - Non-retryable: other 4xx (400/401/403/404/etc)
  - Network/transport errors are retryable (DNS, connection reset, TLS handshake, fetch abort not initiated by user)
- Request ID propagation:
  - Successful responses: the Response wrapper exposes requestId.
  - Error responses: requestId is extracted from headers first, then JSON body fields (request_id/requestId) if present.
- Observability hooks:
  - Retry loop now increments a local attempt counter and attaches it to the error as metadata when we eventually give up.
  - Ensures we do NOT include the Authorization header or API key in any error message (Security review guidance).

Notes / decisions
- 501 handling: The standard doc calls out 5xx generally as retryable; SRE feedback was to treat 501 as non-retryable by default (usually indicates unsupported feature/method rather than transient). This PR implements that as a conservative default; we can revisit once gateway error codes are fully consistent.
- Abort semantics: When the caller’s AbortSignal triggers, we now classify it as non-retryable and preserve the original AbortError name/message.

Testing
- Added unit tests for shouldRetry(): verifies status code matrix and network error classification.
- Added tests ensuring request_id is extracted from:
  - response headers (preferred)
  - JSON error body (fallback)
- Updated existing integration tests to assert error.retryable and error.requestId are present.

Rollout
- This should be non-breaking for most customers: existing code catching Error will continue to work.
- Some customers may have relied on the previous (incorrect) retry-on-400 behavior; this change is intentional and documented in migration notes (tracked in ENG-5128).

Follow-ups
- Retry-After parsing + backoff jitter alignment (PR-1307)
- Timeout defaults + AbortController plumbing (PR-1302)
- Conformance suite fixtures (ENG-5127)

Screenshots/log samples
- Example error (sanitized):
  name=RedwoodAPIError statusCode=429 retryable=true requestId=req_01HR9Q6K9YQG3H2R9M4J7V0Z1A code=RATE_LIMITED

title: sdk(auth): align env var names + precedence with Python/Go (deprecate legacy names)
preview: Summary: Aligns TS SDK authentication environment variables and resolution precedence with the Python and Go SDKs as part of the SDK parity initiative. This change also introduces deprecation warnings for legacy env var names to reduce "works in TS but not in Python" issues and to standardize support/debugging instructions. Motivation: Support + SE feedback shows customers frequently set different env vars per language and are surprised by which value wins when both are present. We’re standardizing on a single set of env var names and a single precedence order across SDKs, with security-reviewed guidance to avoid leaking secrets in logs/errors. New behavior (matches Python/Go): Precedence order: (1) explicit client option (apiKey / bearerToken) (2) environment variables (standard names) (3) none -> throw a typed auth error. Standard env vars supported: REDWOOD_API_KEY for API key auth; REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN for bearer token auth; REDWOOD_BASE_URL remains as-is for endpoint override. Legacy env vars (still accepted, now deprecated with a warning): RW_API_KEY, REDWOOD_API_TOKEN, REDWOOD_TOKEN. If multiple env vars are set: explicit client option always wins; between env vars we prefer REDWOOD_API_KEY over deprecated key vars; bearer token is only used when api key is not present (same as other SDKs). Deprecation warnings: Warnings are emitted once per process (guarded) and do not print secret values. We include guidance text pointing to the migration notes and the new standard names. Error handling: Missing credentials now throws RedwoodAuthError with message safe for logs; error includes "source" metadata (explicit/env/none) but never includes the credential string. Tests: Adds unit coverage for precedence, deprecation warning behavior, and non-leakage. Also updates the README auth snippet to reference REDWOOD_API_KEY / REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN consistently. Notes: This PR intentionally does not change header formats beyond existing behavior (Authorization: Bearer ...). This also keeps backwards compat: legacy env vars will continue to work for at least 2 minor versions; removal will be tracked separately after customers have had time to migrate. Related: ENG-5122 (env var alignment), INT-1182 (better error metadata / support triage).

Standard env vars supported: REDWOOD_API_KEY for API key auth; REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN for bearer token auth; REDWOOD_BASE_URL remains as-is for endpoint override. Legacy env vars (still accepted, now deprecated with a warning): RW_API_KEY, REDWOOD_API_TOKEN, REDWOOD_TOKEN. If multiple env vars are set: explicit client option always wins; between env vars we prefer REDWOOD_API_KEY over deprecated key vars; bearer token is only used when api key is not present (same as other SDKs). Deprecation warnings: Warnings are emitted once per process (guarded) and do not print secret values. We include guidance text pointing to the migration notes and the new standard names. Error handling: Missing credentials now throws RedwoodAuthError with message safe for logs; error includes "source" metadata (explicit/env/none) but never includes the credential string. Tests: Adds unit coverage for precedence, deprecation warning behavior, and non-leakage. Also updates the README auth snippet to reference REDWOOD_API_KEY / REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN consistently. Notes: This PR intentionally does not change header formats beyond existing behavior (Authorization: Bearer ...). This also keeps backwards compat: legacy env vars will continue to work for at least 2 minor versions; removal will be tracked separately after customers have had time to migrate. Related: ENG-5122 (env var alignment), INT-1182 (better error metadata / support triage).

title: Expose typed overload error classes + Retry-After handling docs
preview:
Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding rollout, the API is returning stable overload semantics (HTTP 429/503 with a JSON error payload + correlation IDs + Retry-After guidance). In the TS SDK we currently throw a generic ApiError, which forces customers to string-match messages and often leads to unsafe retry loops.

This PR adds typed overload error classes and doc snippets so client code can (a) branch on stable error codes and (b) implement bounded retries with jitter that respects Retry-After.

What changed: 1) New error types in the SDK: - OverloadError (base) extends ApiError and exposes: status, code, message, correlationId, requestId, retryAfterSeconds (parsed from header), and rawPayload (best-effort). - RateLimitedError: thrown for HTTP 429 when error code indicates throttling / quota / concurrency cap. - ServiceOverloadedError: thrown for HTTP 503 when error code indicates capacity / shed / breaker open.

2) Error parsing: - Parse standard Redwood error payload fields when present: error.code, error.message, error.correlation_id, error.request_id. - Parse Retry-After header (seconds). If missing/invalid, retryAfterSeconds is undefined; the docs recommend a safe client-side backoff floor.

3) Docs + examples: - Added a short “Retry contract” section to docs with a concrete example of exponential backoff + full jitter, respecting Retry-After when provided. - Explicit guidance to avoid retry storms: cap retries, add jitter, and do not immediately retry streaming requests without backoff.

4) Tests: - Unit tests for mapping status + error.code -> typed error class. - Coverage for Retry-After parsing, including header absent and malformed cases.

Notes: - This PR is intentionally additive and does not change the default request retry behavior of the SDK (we don’t auto-retry by default). - The error code set aligns with the overload taxonomy work (see ENG-4822). We treat unknown overload-ish 429/503 as the base OverloadError to keep forward compatibility.

Customer-facing example:
try { await client.chat.completions.create(...); } catch (err) { if (err instanceof ServiceOverloadedError) { const delay = err.retryAfterSeconds ? err.retryAfterSeconds * 1000 : 250; await sleep(jitter(delay)); /* bounded retry */ } }

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Consider adding an optional built-in retry helper in the SDK (behind a flag) once the API rollout stabilizes in more regions. - Add an “idempotency key” example for non-streaming requests once the API docs are updated.

title: Compact connection throttle + async iterable sugar; cross-SDK retry presets and version bump
preview:
Context: Customer workloads and internal benchmarks showed bursty micro-batch patterns causing head-of-line connection contention and surprising ordering latency on long-lived streaming consumers. This PR introduces a compact connection throttle (lightweight per-host connection window + soft drop) in the TypeScript SDK, a set of ergonomic async-iterable helpers for streaming consumers, and a curated set of retry/backoff presets to align behavior across TS/Python/Go. The changes are intentionally additive and backwards-compatible for common call patterns; an opt-in config surface is provided for teams wanting deterministic throttle windows and pre-warmed connectors.

Motivation: Reduce tail latency spikes during high-concurrency streaming usage, simplify stream consumer code across codebases, and provide consistent retry semantics across SDKs so server-side rollouts and client-side fallbacks behave similarly. ENG-1987 captures the production regression we observed; ENG-2123 is the follow-up to standardize retry profiles. SUP-1245 required pragmatic changes in TS first to unblock a customer using mixed-language microservices.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a compact connection throttle implemented at the connection pool layer: light-weight token window per-origin, soft-drop policy when window exhausted, and optional jittered refill. Default disabled; opt-in via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.
- Adds asyncIterable.fromStream and asyncIterable.mapAsync helpers that normalize chunk framing, apply backpressure-aware reads, and expose cancelable iteration semantics. These reduce user boilerplate for streaming handlers.
- Introduces three retry presets: fast-retry, balanced, and conservative, with documented token-based default backoffs and idempotency guidance. Presets are available on ClientConfig.retryProfile.
- Adds version bump notes and a migration guide (docs/migration-2.4.0.md) with suggested changes for app teams and Python/Go sync checklist.
- Unit/integration tests for throttle behavior, iterator cancellation, and retry preset selection.

Files changed (high-level):
- src/connection/compactThrottle.ts (new)
- src/connection/pool.ts (minor refactor + opt-in hooks)
- src/stream/asyncIterable.ts (new)
- src/client/config.ts (new options)
- src/retry/presets.ts (new)
- docs/migration-2.4.0.md (new)
- test/unit/compactThrottle.test.ts (new)
- test/integration/streamCancellation.test.ts (new)
- package.json (version -> 2.4.0)
- README and changelog updates

Commits (summary):
- feat(connection): compact connection throttle implementation and integration tests
- feat(stream): asyncIterable helpers (fromStream, mapAsync, withCancel)
- feat(retry): add retry presets and docs
- feat(config): exposed ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle & retryProfile
- docs: migration guide and examples that show cross-SDK parity guidance
- test: end-to-end test that reproduces pre-fix tail latency pattern

Review notes and decisions:
- Throttle default: disabled. We decided to make the throttle opt-in because some customers rely on aggressive client-side concurrency; enabling by default would risk impacting throughput for those cases. If downstream service agrees, we can flip to on in a later minor.
- Jitter and refill params: conservative defaults to avoid synchronized refills; teams can tune via ClientConfig.compactConnectionThrottle.windowMs and refillJitter.
- Retry presets documented with explicit mapping guidance for Python/Go equivalents (see migration doc). The intention is that each language exposes the same three presets with comparable timings; Python/Go PR checklists are included so maintainers can port values without surprises.

Test and CI: All unit tests and integration tests in the TS repo pass locally and in CI. The PR ran the following checks: lint, build, unit, integration, e2e-stream, and cross-sdk-ci (lightweight smoke). A flaky e2e-stream test failed on first CI run (timing-related); I adjusted test timeouts and added a deterministic clock for the failing test, then retriggered CI. Final CI status: pass.

Review conversation (high level):
- Luca: asked for clearer failure semantics when throttle soft-drops chunks; requested that errors surface with a typed ThrottleError so consumers can decide whether to retry or fallback.
- Maya (author): implemented ThrottleError with code 'E_COMPACT_THROTTLE' and added a short section in the docs describing recommended handling.
- Priya: requested API ergonomics for asyncIterable.mapAsync to accept both sync and async map functions and to preserve chunk identity when map throws. Priya also wanted better cancellation signaling across underlying WS/socket backpressure.
- Maya: updated mapAsync to accept both and added withCancel wrapper to propagate cancellation to upstream socket reads.
- Ethan: validated presets and asked for an explicit callout that these presets are best-effort and servers retain final authority.

Cross-SDK coordination: This PR is the TypeScript implementation. I included a migration checklist and a JSON spec file (docs/compat/retry-presets-2.4.0.json) that describes the three presets (timeouts, multipliers, maxAttempts) to make it straightforward for Python and Go maintainers to port. I communicated this to the SDK channels and opened companion PR templates for redwood-sdk-go and redwood-sdk-python listing the files to change and tests to add. Those companion PRs are referenced in the migration doc and targeted for the 2.4.0 family.

Backward compatibility and stability: No public API surface was removed. The new helpers and throttle are additive; default behavior remains unchanged unless a team opts in to the throttle or selects a retry preset. Tests include round-trip examples that ensure existing simple usages (client.chat.generate, simple streaming consumers) behave identically.

Manual test checklist performed before merge:
- Local integration harness with 100 concurrent stream consumers verified no regressions in throughput for non-throttle config.
- Enabled compact throttle in a staging deploy and reproduced reduced tail P99 for artificial burst test.
- Verified asyncIterable cancellation closes underlying socket and frees KV caches.
- Verified packaged SDK bump in an internal demo service compiled and ran.

Merge outcome: Squash merged to main after final approvals from Luca and Priya. CI green at merge.

Follow-ups and rollout plan:
- Open follow-up to discuss default-on strategy after customer telemetry (ENG-2190).
- Coordinate Python/Go PRs to land equivalent presets and iterator helpers (owners: @go-sdk-team, @py-sdk-team).
- Add telemetry for throttle drop events to Console as a low-volume metric (SRE/observability ticket included in the migration doc).

title: introduce-compact-auth-orchestrator-and-multi-sdk-stream-ergonomics
preview: Motivation: multiple SDKs had divergent credential-warmup and stream-consumer ergonomics which leaked complexity into application code. This PR introduces a compact auth orchestrator interface and runtime shim that 1) centralizes credential warmup/prefetch logic, 2) exposes a minimal async pull-stream ergonomic facade across TypeScript/ Python/ Go and 3) ships a set of opinionated retry presets tuned for low-latency streaming use-cases. The change is intended to reduce duplicate logic in application-level code and improve parity across SDKs. Summary of changes: -- Introduce compact auth orchestrator (ts): src/auth/orchestrator.ts, with a light-weight in-memory cache, lease hints, and prefetch hooks for token providers. The orchestrator exposes a small surface used by streaming connectors and session facades. -- Add pullstream ergonomic facade (ts): src/stream/pullstream.ts and public exports in lib/index.ts. This provides a simpler async iterator consumer with built-in backoff and checkpoint hints. -- Cross-SDK compatibility: added step-by-step shim examples in examples/multiproto-auth/ demonstrating TypeScript -> Python -> Go usage patterns and fixture scripts used in CI. -- Retry presets: packages/config/retries.ts and docs/retry-presets.md document 'low-latency', 'throughput', and 'conservative' presets used by the runtime. -- Version bumps: package.json updated to 1.8.0; docs updated. -- Tests: added unit tests for orchestrator, integration tests for streaming recovery scenarios, and CI harness changes to run cross-sdk smoke tests in the monorepo pipeline. Migration notes: existing consumer code that called internal credential helpers should switch to new orchestrator.getLeaseHint() or use the facade helper from lib/pullstream; however default behaviour preserves legacy token-provider calls so most apps will not require immediate changes. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4321) [x] unit + integration tests [x] examples updated [x] docs + changelog entry [x] version bumps for TS/Go/Py (packaging handled in separate release PR). How tested: local unit + integration suite, nightly cross-sdk pipeline, manual verification with a sample chat app using long-running streaming sessions. CI: flakey e2e in first run (timeout in cross-language smoke) was addressed by increasing the test harness timeout and adding a small token prewarm step in the CI job. Performance: no measurable regression on small-scale benchmarks; small latency improvement observed in streaming reconnect paths due to prefetch. Rollout: merged to main and released in the next SDK patch cycle.

This PR introduces a compact auth orchestrator interface and runtime shim that 1) centralizes credential warmup/prefetch logic, 2) exposes a minimal async pull-stream ergonomic facade across TypeScript/ Python/ Go and 3) ships a set of opinionated retry presets tuned for low-latency streaming use-cases. The change is intended to reduce duplicate logic in application-level code and improve parity across SDKs. Summary of changes: -- Introduce compact auth orchestrator (ts): src/auth/orchestrator.ts, with a light-weight in-memory cache, lease hints, and prefetch hooks for token providers. The orchestrator exposes a small surface used by streaming connectors and session facades. -- Add pullstream ergonomic facade (ts): src/stream/pullstream.ts and public exports in lib/index.ts. This provides a simpler async iterator consumer with built-in backoff and checkpoint hints. -- Cross-SDK compatibility: added step-by-step shim examples in examples/multiproto-auth/ demonstrating TypeScript -> Python -> Go usage patterns and fixture scripts used in CI. -- Retry presets: packages/config/retries.ts and docs/retry-presets.md document 'low-latency', 'throughput', and 'conservative' presets used by the runtime. -- Version bumps: package.json updated to 1.8.0; docs updated. -- Tests: added unit tests for orchestrator, integration tests for streaming recovery scenarios, and CI harness changes to run cross-sdk smoke tests in the monorepo pipeline. Migration notes: existing consumer code that called internal credential helpers should switch to new orchestrator.getLeaseHint() or use the facade helper from lib/pullstream; however default behaviour preserves legacy token-provider calls so most apps will not require immediate changes. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4321) [x] unit + integration tests [x] examples updated [x] docs + changelog entry [x] version bumps for TS/Go/Py (packaging handled in separate release PR). How tested: local unit + integration suite, nightly cross-sdk pipeline, manual verification with a sample chat app using long-running streaming sessions. CI: flakey e2e in first run (timeout in cross-language smoke) was addressed by increasing the test harness timeout and adding a small token prewarm step in the CI job. Performance: no measurable regression on small-scale benchmarks; small latency improvement observed in streaming reconnect paths due to prefetch. Rollout: merged to main and released in the next SDK patch cycle.

title: SDK: add experimentStickyKey option + expose assigned variant on responses
preview: Context: Route-level experiments require deterministic assignment. The platform supports a stickiness key (preferred: stable per end-user or per tenant) that the router uses when bucketing a request into a variant. This PR adds an optional experimentStickyKey field to the TS SDK request options and includes small helpers to read the assigned {experiment_id, variant_id} from response headers so customers can log/debug assignments. Summary of changes: 1) New request option: experimentStickyKey?: string on chat/completions/embeddings/rerank request option types. When present, the SDK sends header X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key (exact casing handled by fetch). 2) New response metadata: the SDK now captures experiment assignment headers (x-redwood-experiment-id, x-redwood-experiment-variant, x-redwood-experiment-config-version) and exposes them via response.experimental?.assignment for non-streaming calls. 3) Streaming support: for stream responses, assignment headers are available immediately from the initial response; we surface them on the returned stream handle as stream.experimental.assignment and also provide an onResponse callback hook for callers that want to log headers before consuming tokens. 4) Ergonomics: add helper parseExperimentAssignment(headers) and type ExperimentAssignment to avoid callers hand-parsing header names. 5) Docs + examples: update README snippets for route experiments usage and add a troubleshooting note (stickiness key should be high-entropy; do not default to IP; prefer customer_user_id or customer_session_id). Backwards compatibility: option is fully optional and does not change behavior when omitted. No breaking changes to existing response shapes (new fields are additive under .experimental). Testing: unit tests for header injection + parsing, and one integration-style test with mocked fetch verifying streaming handle includes assignment. Notes: header names align with platform OTel conventions and ADR-047/048; the SDK intentionally does not attempt to generate a default stickiness key to avoid low-entropy skew (see recent beta issue).

title: Fix header merge for streaming in Edge runtime (preserve Authorization)
preview: Context: SUP-4884 (customer reports 403 when streaming from Next.js Edge). Root cause: in the streaming helper we constructed RequestInit twice and used object spread on headers; in Edge, Headers behaves differently and Authorization was dropped during the second merge. Changes: use a single canonical Headers instance; ensure Authorization is always set when apiKey is configured; add integration test using the edge fetch polyfill; update docs with edge runtime note. Release: v1.4.1.
